exercises.title: What does kubectl scale do?
exercises.instructions:
Payment service running with 3 replicas. Command: `kubectl scale deployment payment-service --replicas=10`
1. How many pods after command?
2. How long until all 10 are ready?
3. Does this cause downtime?
exercises.expected_solution:
1. 10 pods (7 new + 3 existing)
2. Time: ~30-60 seconds (each pod needs to start Spring Boot ~25s + readiness probe initialDelay 30s)
3. No downtime: existing 3 pods continue serving traffic while 7 new ones start.
   Once each new pod passes readiness, it joins the Service endpoint list.
   Traffic is load-balanced across all healthy pods (up to 10 eventually).

test_cases.expected:
1. 10 pods (7 new + 3 existing)
2. Time: ~30-60 seconds (each pod needs to start Spring Boot ~25s + readiness probe initialDelay 30s)
3. No downtime: existing 3 pods continue serving traffic while 7 new ones start.
   Once each new pod passes readiness, it joins the Service endpoint list.
   Traffic is load-balanced across all healthy pods (up to 10 eventually).

hints: Scaling up: new pods start while old ones serve traffic
Readiness probe: new pod only receives traffic after probe passes
Scale down: existing pods terminated one by one (maxUnavailable applies)

exercises.title: Complete the Deployment spec
exercises.instructions: Fill in resource requests/limits and probe paths.
exercises.starter_code:
resources:
  requests:
    memory: '___Mi'
    cpu: '250m'
  limits:
    memory: '768Mi'
    cpu: '500m'
readinessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /actuator/health/___
    port: 8080
  initialDelaySeconds: ___
livenessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /actuator/health/___
    port: 8080
exercises.expected_solution:
resources:
  requests:
    memory: '512Mi'
    cpu: '250m'
  limits:
    memory: '768Mi'
    cpu: '500m'
readinessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /actuator/health/readiness
    port: 8080
  initialDelaySeconds: 30
livenessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /actuator/health/liveness
    port: 8080

blanks.hint: Spring Boot startup time in seconds

hints: readiness: are-you-ready-for-traffic
liveness: are-you-still-alive
initialDelaySeconds: wait for Spring Boot to finish starting

exercises.title: Write a ClusterIP Service YAML
exercises.instructions:
Write a Kubernetes Service for payment-service:
1. ClusterIP type (internal)
2. Selects pods with label app: payment-service
3. Service port 80 → container port 8080
4. Namespace: payments
exercises.expected_solution:
# k8s/service.yml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: payment-service
  namespace: payments
  labels:
    app: payment-service
spec:
  selector:
    app: payment-service    # routes to pods with this label
  ports:
  - name: http
    protocol: TCP
    port: 80                # service port (what clients call)
    targetPort: 8080        # container port (where Spring Boot listens)
  type: ClusterIP           # internal-only DNS

# After apply:
# Other pods reach it via DNS: http://payment-service.payments.svc.cluster.local
# Or short form (same namespace): http://payment-service
hints: ClusterIP: default type, internal cluster DNS only
selector must match pod labels exactly
port 80 → 8080: common pattern (standard HTTP port → Spring Boot)

exercises.title: Write a complete Deployment YAML for Zerodha
exercises.instructions:
Write complete Deployment YAML for Zerodha order-service:
1. 3 replicas, namespace: trading
2. Resources: 512Mi/768Mi, 250m/500m
3. Readiness + liveness probes
4. Rolling update: maxSurge=1, maxUnavailable=0
5. Env: SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=production
exercises.expected_solution:
# k8s/order-service-deployment.yml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: order-service
  namespace: trading
  labels:
    app: order-service
    team: trading
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: order-service
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 1
      maxUnavailable: 0
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: order-service
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: order-service
        image: zerodha/order-service:latest
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8080
        env:
        - name: SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE
          value: production
        resources:
          requests: { memory: '512Mi', cpu: '250m' }
          limits:   { memory: '768Mi', cpu: '500m' }
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet: { path: /actuator/health/readiness, port: 8080 }
          initialDelaySeconds: 30
          periodSeconds: 10
          failureThreshold: 3
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet: { path: /actuator/health/liveness, port: 8080 }
          initialDelaySeconds: 60
          periodSeconds: 30
          failureThreshold: 3
hints: failureThreshold: 3 means 3 consecutive failures before action
periodSeconds: how often to run the probe
initialDelaySeconds on liveness should be > readiness (more time to warm up)

exercises.title: kubectl commands for common operations
exercises.instructions:
Write kubectl commands for:
1. Deploy payment-service from YAML
2. Check rollout status
3. Watch pods in real-time
4. Check pod logs
5. Scale to 5 replicas
6. Roll back to previous version
7. Describe a pod (for debugging)
exercises.expected_solution:
# DEPLOY:
kubectl apply -f k8s/deployment.yml -f k8s/service.yml
# Or apply entire directory:
kubectl apply -f k8s/

# CHECK ROLLOUT STATUS (blocks until complete):
kubectl rollout status deployment/payment-service -n payments
# Output: 'deployment "payment-service" successfully rolled out'

# WATCH PODS IN REAL-TIME:
kubectl get pods -n payments -w
# Shows: NAME  READY  STATUS  RESTARTS  AGE (updates live)

# POD LOGS:
kubectl logs -f deployment/payment-service -n payments
# Follow (-f) all pods in the deployment
kubectl logs payment-service-abc123 -n payments --tail=100
# Specific pod, last 100 lines

# SCALE:
kubectl scale deployment payment-service --replicas=5 -n payments

# ROLLBACK TO PREVIOUS VERSION:
kubectl rollout undo deployment/payment-service -n payments
# Rollback to specific revision:
kubectl rollout undo deployment/payment-service --to-revision=2 -n payments

# DESCRIBE POD (detailed info + events for debugging):
kubectl describe pod payment-service-abc123 -n payments
# Shows: events, resource usage, probe results, restart reason
hints: kubectl apply -f: creates or updates resources (idempotent)
rollout status: blocks until deployment completes (useful in CI)
rollout undo: fast rollback to previous ReplicaSet (seconds, not minutes)

exercises.title: Readiness vs liveness probe — when each fires
exercises.instructions:
Explain the difference with 3 concrete Spring Boot scenarios:
1. App starting (Flyway migrations running)
2. Visa API is down (circuit breaker OPEN)
3. App deadlocked (all threads stuck)
4. Write the probe config for each scenario
exercises.expected_solution:
# SCENARIO 1: App starting (Flyway migrations running ~15 seconds)
# Probe behavior:
#   Readiness probe FAILS (app not ready — Flyway running)
#   → Pod not added to Service endpoints (no traffic yet)
#   → After Flyway completes + Spring Boot initialized → readiness passes
#   → Pod starts receiving traffic
#   Liveness probe: not checked yet (initialDelaySeconds=60 not elapsed)
#
# Configuration:
#   readinessProbe.initialDelaySeconds: 30  (wait for startup)
#   readinessProbe.failureThreshold: 5      (allow several failures during startup)

# SCENARIO 2: Visa API down (CB OPEN)
# Probe behavior:
#   Readiness probe depends on Spring Boot health config:
#   If you add CircuitBreakerHealthIndicator AND mark it as readiness-affecting:
#     Readiness FAILS → pod removed from endpoints (won't receive payment traffic)
#   Default: /actuator/health/readiness only checks required components (DB, etc.)
#   For Visa CB: typically don't fail readiness (CB is a feature, not broken app)
#   Handle in application logic (fallback returns degraded result)
#   Liveness: NOT affected (app is running correctly, just degraded)

# SCENARIO 3: App deadlocked (all 200 threads stuck)
# Probe behavior:
#   Liveness probe: attempts GET /actuator/health/liveness
#   All threads stuck → request times out → probe FAILS
#   After failureThreshold (e.g., 3) failures → k8s RESTARTS the pod
#   → deadlock cleared → service recovers automatically
#   Readiness probe: also fails (same reason)
#   → pod removed from traffic during failing period

# application.yml for k8s probes:
# management:
#   health:
#     probes:
#       enabled: true    # enables /readiness and /liveness distinct endpoints
#   endpoint:
#     health:
#       group:
#         readiness:
#           include: readinessState,db,redis  # what readiness checks
#         liveness:
#           include: livenessState            # just is-app-alive

public class ProbeScenarios { public static void main(String[] a) {
    System.out.println("Readiness: traffic gate. Liveness: restart trigger."); } }

expected_stdout_contains: Readiness: traffic gate. Liveness: restart trigger.

hints: Readiness failure: removed from load balancer (no new traffic)
Liveness failure: RESTARTED (clears deadlocks, stuck threads)
management.health.probes.enabled=true: separate /readiness and /liveness endpoints

exercises.title: HorizontalPodAutoscaler for traffic spikes
exercises.instructions:
Write HPA for Zerodha order service:
1. Scale based on CPU: target 50% utilization
2. Min 2 replicas, max 20 replicas
3. Scale-up cooldown: 60s, scale-down: 300s
4. Explain: why scale down slowly?
exercises.expected_solution:
# k8s/order-service-hpa.yml
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: order-service-hpa
  namespace: trading
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: order-service
  minReplicas: 2      # always at least 2 (for HA during off-hours)
  maxReplicas: 20     # cost cap: never more than 20 pods
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 50  # scale when avg CPU > 50%
  behavior:
    scaleUp:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 60  # wait 60s before scaling up again
      policies:
      - type: Pods
        value: 4    # add at most 4 pods at a time
        periodSeconds: 60
    scaleDown:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 300  # wait 5 min before scaling down
      policies:
      - type: Pods
        value: 1    # remove at most 1 pod at a time
        periodSeconds: 60

# WHY SCALE DOWN SLOWLY (300s window, 1 pod at a time):
# Stock trading: market open at 9:15am, close at 3:30pm
# At 3:30pm: CPU drops instantly as trading stops
# If scale down is fast: all pods terminated immediately
# If traffic returns at 3:31pm (corporate announcements): no pods ready → 502s
# Slow scale down (300s): keeps pods for 5 minutes after traffic drops
# Safety net for unpredictable traffic rebounds
hints: minReplicas: 2 for HA (1 can restart while other serves traffic)
averageUtilization: average across all pods in deployment
stabilizationWindow: prevents flapping (scale up/down repeatedly)

exercises.title: Multi-service k8s: order-service calls payment-service
exercises.instructions:
Order-service calls payment-service in Kubernetes:
1. RestTemplate in order-service using k8s service DNS
2. Service YAML for both
3. Namespace-qualified DNS name
4. How does k8s load balance across payment-service pods?
hints: k8s DNS: service-name.namespace.svc.cluster.local
ClusterIP: stable IP that routes to healthy pods (updated via kube-proxy)
Use @Value for service URL: allows override in different environments

exercises.title: Kubernetes namespace strategy for Razorpay
exercises.instructions:
Design namespace strategy:
1. Create namespaces: payments, trading, infra
2. ResourceQuota for payments namespace: max 20 pods, 10Gi memory
3. NetworkPolicy: payments pods only accept traffic from trading namespace
4. Explain: why namespaces for isolation?
exercises.expected_solution:
# k8s/namespaces.yml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: payments
  labels:
    team: payments-engineering
    env: production
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: trading
  labels:
    team: trading-engineering
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: infra
  labels:
    team: platform

---
# ResourceQuota: prevent runaway scaling in payments namespace
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: payments-quota
  namespace: payments
spec:
  hard:
    pods: '20'              # max 20 pods
    requests.memory: 5Gi   # total requested memory
    limits.memory: 10Gi    # total limit memory
    requests.cpu: '5'      # 5 total CPU cores requested

---
# NetworkPolicy: only trading namespace can call payment-service
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: payment-ingress-policy
  namespace: payments
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: payment-service
  policyTypes: [Ingress]
  ingress:
  - from:
    - namespaceSelector:
        matchLabels:
          team: trading-engineering  # only trading namespace
    ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 8080

# WHY NAMESPACES:
# Isolation: payments team can't accidentally delete trading pods
# ResourceQuota: prevents runaway scaling costs
# NetworkPolicy: PCI-DSS compliance — payment service only reachable from authorized services
# RBAC: payments team has full access to 'payments' namespace only
hints: ResourceQuota: prevents one team from consuming all cluster resources
NetworkPolicy: default-deny then allow specific sources
Namespace labels: used by NetworkPolicy namespaceSelector

exercises.title: Rolling update: what happens step by step?
exercises.instructions:
Deployment: 3 replicas. Strategy: maxSurge=1, maxUnavailable=0.
Current image: v1.0. New image: v2.0.

Describe the exact sequence of events during the rolling update.
exercises.expected_solution:
Rolling update sequence (3 replicas, maxSurge=1, maxUnavailable=0):

T=0: kubectl apply (new image v2.0)
  Current state: [v1.0-pod1, v1.0-pod2, v1.0-pod3] (all ready, all serving traffic)

T=5s: k8s creates 1 new pod (maxSurge=1: can have 4 pods total during update)
  State: [v1.0-pod1, v1.0-pod2, v1.0-pod3, v2.0-pod4-starting]
  Traffic: still going to all 3 v1.0 pods

T=35s: v2.0-pod4 passes readiness probe (initialDelaySeconds=30)
  State: [v1.0-pod1, v1.0-pod2, v1.0-pod3, v2.0-pod4-READY]
  Traffic: split 4 ways (3×v1 + 1×v2)

T=36s: k8s terminates v1.0-pod1 (maxUnavailable=0: can terminate now — still have 3 ready)
  State: [v1.0-pod2, v1.0-pod3, v2.0-pod4] (3 ready = desired replicas)
  Then immediately starts v2.0-pod5

T=40s → T=75s: v2.0-pod5 starts, becomes ready, v1.0-pod2 terminated
  State: [v1.0-pod3, v2.0-pod4, v2.0-pod5]

T=75s → T=110s: v2.0-pod6 starts, becomes ready, v1.0-pod3 terminated
  Final state: [v2.0-pod4, v2.0-pod5, v2.0-pod6] ← all v2.0!

Total time: ~110 seconds for 3-replica deployment
Downtime: 0 — always ≥ 3 ready pods serving traffic

test_cases.expected:
Rolling update sequence (3 replicas, maxSurge=1, maxUnavailable=0):

T=0: kubectl apply (new image v2.0)
  Current state: [v1.0-pod1, v1.0-pod2, v1.0-pod3] (all ready, all serving traffic)

T=5s: k8s creates 1 new pod (maxSurge=1: can have 4 pods total during update)
  State: [v1.0-pod1, v1.0-pod2, v1.0-pod3, v2.0-pod4-starting]
  Traffic: still going to all 3 v1.0 pods

T=35s: v2.0-pod4 passes readiness probe (initialDelaySeconds=30)
  State: [v1.0-pod1, v1.0-pod2, v1.0-pod3, v2.0-pod4-READY]
  Traffic: split 4 ways (3×v1 + 1×v2)

T=36s: k8s terminates v1.0-pod1 (maxUnavailable=0: can terminate now — still have 3 ready)
  State: [v1.0-pod2, v1.0-pod3, v2.0-pod4] (3 ready = desired replicas)
  Then immediately starts v2.0-pod5

T=40s → T=75s: v2.0-pod5 starts, becomes ready, v1.0-pod2 terminated
  State: [v1.0-pod3, v2.0-pod4, v2.0-pod5]

T=75s → T=110s: v2.0-pod6 starts, becomes ready, v1.0-pod3 terminated
  Final state: [v2.0-pod4, v2.0-pod5, v2.0-pod6] ← all v2.0!

Total time: ~110 seconds for 3-replica deployment
Downtime: 0 — always ≥ 3 ready pods serving traffic

hints: maxSurge=1: can temporarily have 4 pods (3 desired + 1 new)
maxUnavailable=0: never terminate old until new is ready
initialDelaySeconds=30: determines when new pod becomes 'ready' and old can be killed

exercises.title: Spring Boot k8s readiness/liveness config
exercises.instructions:
Configure Spring Boot for Kubernetes health probes:
1. Enable distinct /readiness and /liveness endpoints
2. Readiness group: include DB + Redis health
3. Liveness group: include only livenessState (not DB — DB down ≠ need restart)
4. @SpringBootTest: verify both endpoints return UP
5. Show: why DB failure shouldn't trigger liveness (restart won't fix DB)
hints: management.health.probes.enabled=true: key config for k8s-style probes
Liveness only livenessState: never include DB in liveness (infinite restart loop!)
Readiness includes db,redis: if dependencies down → no traffic (correct)

exercises.title: Debug failing deployment: pod stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
exercises.instructions:
Pod shows CrashLoopBackOff after deployment.
Steps to diagnose:
1. kubectl commands to find the problem
2. Common causes for Spring Boot pods in CrashLoopBackOff
3. Fix for 'OOMKilled' vs 'Error 1' (application exception)
4. How to get logs from a crashed pod
exercises.expected_solution:
// CRASHLOOPBACKOFF DIAGNOSIS:

// Step 1: Identify the problem
String step1 = '''
  # Check pod status:
  kubectl get pods -n payments
  # Shows: payment-service-xyz  0/1  CrashLoopBackOff  5  3m

  # Get pod details (look at 'Last State' and 'Events'):
  kubectl describe pod payment-service-xyz -n payments
  # Look for:
  #   Last State: Terminated → Reason: OOMKilled  → Memory issue
  #   Last State: Terminated → Exit Code: 1       → App exception
  #   Events: Back-off restarting failed container
''';

// Step 2: Get logs from crashed pod
String step2 = '''
  # Previous container logs (from before crash):
  kubectl logs payment-service-xyz -n payments --previous
  # --previous: gets logs from the LAST crashed instance
  # Look for: Exception, OOM, startup failure

  # If multiple containers:
  kubectl logs payment-service-xyz -n payments --previous -c payment-service
''';

// Step 3: Common causes and fixes
String step3 = '''
  CAUSE 1: OOMKilled
    kubectl describe pod shows: Last State: Terminated, Reason: OOMKilled
    Fix: increase memory limits or reduce MaxRAMPercentage
    resources.limits.memory: 768Mi → 1Gi

  CAUSE 2: DB connection failed on startup
    Logs: BeanCreationException: datasource connection refused
    Fix: check DB service name, DB readiness, credentials
    kubectl get service postgres -n payments  # does service exist?

  CAUSE 3: Missing ConfigMap/Secret
    Logs: IllegalStateException: Could not resolve placeholder '${DB_PASSWORD}'
    Fix: create the ConfigMap/Secret before deploying
    kubectl create secret generic payment-secrets --from-literal=db_password=xxx

  CAUSE 4: Wrong image tag
    kubectl describe: ErrImagePull or ImagePullBackOff
    Fix: check image registry credentials, tag exists in registry
''';

public class K8sDebug { public static void main(String[] a) {
    System.out.println("CrashLoopBackOff: kubectl describe + logs --previous"); } }
hints: --previous: essential for crash debugging (current container has no logs if crashed immediately)
kubectl describe: Events section shows k8s-level problems
OOMKilled vs Exit Code 1: different causes, different fixes

exercises.title: Complete Razorpay k8s deployment with all components
exercises.instructions:
Build complete Razorpay k8s setup:
1. Deployment: 3 replicas, resources, probes, rolling update
2. ClusterIP Service
3. HPA: scale on CPU, min 2 max 10
4. Spring Boot k8s health probe config
5. @SpringBootTest: 2 tests — readiness UP, liveness UP

All tests pass.
hints: management.health.probes.enabled=true: key for separate readiness/liveness
Both probes return 200 UP in test: no real DB needed for livenessState
Readiness may return DOWN in test without DB — use is2xxSuccessful() not isOk()

exercises.title: PodDisruptionBudget for zero-downtime maintenance
exercises.instructions:
Write PodDisruptionBudget for payment-service:
1. Ensure at least 2 pods always available during maintenance
2. Apply to payment-service in payments namespace
3. Explain: how PDB enables k8s node drain without downtime
4. Show: kubectl drain command and PDB interaction
exercises.expected_solution:
# k8s/payment-pdb.yml
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: payment-service-pdb
  namespace: payments
spec:
  minAvailable: 2    # always keep at least 2 pods available
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: payment-service

# Alternative: maxUnavailable: 1 (at most 1 pod can be unavailable)
# With 3 replicas: minAvailable=2 means same as maxUnavailable=1

# HOW PDB ENABLES SAFE NODE DRAIN:
# Scenario: node maintenance (OS patch, hardware replacement)
# Without PDB:
#   kubectl drain node1 → all pods on node1 evicted immediately
#   If 2/3 payment pods were on node1: only 1 pod serving → overloaded!
#
# With PDB (minAvailable=2):
#   kubectl drain node1 → k8s checks PDB before evicting each pod
#   k8s evicts payment-pod-a → 2 pods remain (≥ minAvailable) → OK
#   k8s tries to evict payment-pod-b → only 2 remain, would go to 1 → BLOCKED
#   k8s waits for Scheduler to place payment-pod-a on another node
#   Once payment-pod-a is Running+Ready on node2 → evicts payment-pod-b
#   Result: always ≥ 2 pods serving traffic

# kubectl drain with PDB:
# kubectl drain node1 --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data
# (blocks until all pods can be evicted respecting PDBs)

public class PDBDoc { public static void main(String[] a) {
    System.out.println("PDB: prevents evicting too many pods during maintenance"); } }

expected_stdout_contains: PDB: prevents evicting too many pods

hints: minAvailable: minimum pods that must remain available
kubectl drain: evicts pods respecting PDB (blocks if PDB would be violated)
PDB + PodAntiAffinity: distribute pods across nodes for HA

exercises.title: Complete Zerodha k8s trading platform — M21 T2 synthesis
exercises.instructions:
Build complete Zerodha k8s trading setup:
1. Deployment: order-service (3 replicas) + readiness/liveness
2. Service: ClusterIP
3. HPA: scale on CPU (target 50%, min 2, max 20)
4. PDB: minAvailable=2
5. Spring Boot: readiness includes db, liveness only livenessState
6. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): readiness, liveness, metrics endpoint UP

All tests pass.
hints: is2xxSuccessful(): more lenient than isOk() — handles 200 or 503 during test
liveness includes only livenessState: always UP as long as app process is alive
Prometheus endpoint: always contains jvm_memory in any Spring Boot Actuator app
